home.welcome1: Hey there! Welcome to the beginner JavaScript module from SAE Zurich.
home.welcome2: These notes are intended for SAE students only and can be used as a stand alone guide or as a quick reference for all the different parts of JavaScript.
home.welcome3: If you find anything missing or a spelling mistake, please let me know under hadrian.chio@gmail.com

basics.intro1: JavaScript is a lightweight programming language that web developers commonly use to create more dynamic interactions when developing web pages, applications, servers, and or even games.
basics.intro2: Developers generally use JavaScript alongside HTML and CSS The scripting language works well with CSS in formatting HTML elements. However, JavaScript still maintains user interaction, something that CSS cannot do by itself.
basics.intro3: JavaScripts implementations within the web, mobile application, and game development make the scripting language worth learning.
basics.usecase1: It was a general-purpose scripting language to ensure web pages interoperability across different browsers and devices.
basics.usecase2: JavaScript has continued to grow alongside new browsers like Mozilla Firefox and Google Chrome since then. The latter even started developing the first modern JavaScript engine, called V8, which compiles bytecode into native machine code.
basics.usecase3: Today, JavaScript has plenty of frameworks and libraries to simplify complex projects, such as AngularJS, jQuery, and ReactJS.
basics.usecase4: Originally run on the client-side, the JavaScript implementation has branched out to the server-side after the Node.js development ‒ a cross-platform server environment built on the Google Chrome JavaScript V8 engine.
basics.terminalIntro: In this section we will briefly go through your choices of terminal, if Node.js and npm are installed, some command line basics as well a quick intro to the recommended editor setup
basics.terminal1: You can use the built in terminal application which on a Mac you can find under Applications > Utilities > Terminal. I myself am a big fan of the terminal called iTerm, but in the end, they all have the same use case.
basics.terminal2: On Windows, the terminal is called Command Prompt. You can access this by going to Start > All Programs > Accessories > Command Prompt. There is another terminal for Windows called Cmder.
basics.nodejs1: Next up, we need Node.js. If you haven't got it installed yet, install the latest LTS version with the button below👇
basics.nodejs2: We will primarily use Node.js for its tooling, bundling, formatting, etc. So for our sanity we will go ahead and install it.
basics.nodejs3: To check if you have Node.js or what version you have installed, you can check with your terminal ov choice like so:
basics.npm1: Last but not least you have to choose which package manager you are going to use. All of them are more or less the same. NPM is the most known, but it is slighty slower than the other 2. PNPM has the same syntax as NPM but is way faster. The caveat here is, that PNPM doesn't work everywhere, shuch as the deployment from GitHub to Digital Ocean. Personally, I am using Yarn most of the time.
basics.runjs1: That's a good question. to run a JavaScript Script you have 3 different methods. The simplest way is to use the script-tag anywhere in your HTML. But it might get somewhat chaotic once you have a lot of JavaScript code. The other 2 ways are more common. You can either link your script in the HTML-Body or you can link it in the HTML-Head with a defer:
basics.console1: The console is one of the most vital tools you can use when using JavaScript. To access it open the developer tools from your desired browser and navigate to the console. Whatever you write inside a console.log() in your JavaScript file, it will be displayed in the console of the browser. You can also see every error and warning inside the console.
basics.console2: It is insanely useful for debugging purposes. Imagine you're trying to work with an API, but you don't know the structure of the response you get. The console will help you to see the structure of the API and much much more.

variablesAndStatements.intro1: Variables are a building block of JavaScript, and you can't use JavaScript without knowing variables. We will cover what they are, what the different types of them are, what declaration means as well as what a statement is in JavaScript.
variablesAndStatements.intro2: We have three different options to declare a variable in JavaScript and each of them have their own use-case. The three different options are:
variablesAndStatements.varLetConst1: The simplest way to make a general all-purpose variable is to use the type var. The syntax is the following: var variableName = variableValue. You can almost use anything for the variable name, but there are some limitations, which we will discuss in a jiffy.
variablesAndStatements.varLetConst2: We just defined our first variable. We declared it with the name 'greetings' and defined the value of 'Hello World' with the type of a string (we will discuss what a string is in another module).
variablesAndStatements.varLetConst3: To display our freshly defined variable inside the browser console, we have to wrap it inside a console.log() like so: console.log(greetings);. When you properly linked your JavaScript file, defined the variable and as well inserted a console.log(), your Browser will look like this:
variablesAndStatements.varLetConst4: The second way to declare a variable is with the help of 'let' and the syntax looks like this: let variableName = variableValue 👇
variablesAndStatements.varLetConst5: We just defined another variable with the help of let. This time around, the variable name is 'age' and has 55 as a value, which is of type number. You can display the output the same way as you did with the 'greetings' variable with the help of a console.log().
variablesAndStatements.varLetConst6: Lastly we can use const to define a variable. The syntax is similar to the other ones: const variableName = variableValue 👇
variablesAndStatements.varLetConst7: Now we defined const variable with the name of 'amICool' and a boolean type value of true. Let's look at the differences between the three types of variables:
variablesAndStatements.varLetConst8: let and var are mutable, hence the value inside of them can be updated. const on the other hand is immutable, hence it a const variable is a constant variable. Let's look at an example:
variablesAndStatements.varLetConst9: As you can see in the example, you are able to redeclare a var. But let throws an error, if you try to redeclare it, because it's a block-scoped variable (more on that later). The same goes for the const. If you try to redefine a const like cool = false, it will also throw an error, because you are not able to update a constant variable. We will see in another module, that there are some exceptions to const and you can change under certain circumstances in an array or object.
variablesAndStatements.varLetConst10: The important thing to remember is, we have three different ways to declare a variable, and that var and let are mutable and const is immutable.
variablesAndStatements.statements1: Semi-colons are commonly found after every JavaScript line. They are used to indicate that a line has ended and defines a statement
variablesAndStatements.statements2: A statement is an instruction for the computer, browser or JavaScript interpreter to do something. Anytime you're doing something with JavaScript, you are referring to a statement. After each statement, you will need to add a semi-colon to the end of the line or use a code-formatter which does it automatically for you.
variablesAndStatements.codeBlocks1: A code block is different from a statement. While statements only occupy one line, a code block can stretch through unlimited amounts of lines and live inside these curly brackets { } 👇
variablesAndStatements.codeBlocks2: Function definitions, if statements, loop are all code-blocks. In those instances you are running some code with several instructions for the computer inside of the block.

functions.intro1: In JavaScript, a function is a block of code that can be reused multiple times in a program. Functions allow you to divide your code into smaller, more manageable pieces, which can make your code easier to read, understand, and debug. Functions are created using the function keyword, followed by a name for the function and a set of parentheses that may contain one or more parameters 👇
functions.intro2: In this example, the function is named myFunction and it does not take any arguments. Inside the function, you can write any code that you want to be executed when the function is called.
functions.intro3: With the help of functions we can group together a set of statement. As a refresher: a statement is whenever a line ends with the semi-colon ;, which indicates that JavaScript has to do something. Functions can also receive data which are known as arguments. So whenever you pass a data to a function, you pass an argument to the function, which can further be worked with.
functions.intro4: Functions are very versatile as they can perform a set of work (one or multiple statements) and even can return a value.
functions.builtin1:
In-built JavaScript functions are functions that are included with the JavaScript language by default. These functions are part of the JavaScript language and are available for use in your code without you having to define them yourself. Some examples of in-built JavaScript functions include Math.random(), which generates a random number, and console.log(), which outputs data to the console.
These functions are commonly used in JavaScript programs to perform a wide variety of tasks, such as generating random numbers, formatting text, and working with dates and times. The sole purpose of console.log() is to log something on the console and not return a value. That means you can log anything on the console: values, functions, objects, arrays, etc. Not all functions have to return a value as they can only also work off a certain set of commands.
functions.builtin2: Another really interesting built-in function is the Date.now(). What this function does is comething weird: It returns the number of milliseconds that have passed since January 1, 1970. It's a standard to convert those millisecond to a desired timestamp 👇
functions.builtin3: A different built-in function would be the console.clear() function and here's an example how it would be used 👇
functions.builtin4: console.clear() function is called in the middle of the code to clear the console. This means that any text or data that was previously output to the console will be cleared, and the console will be empty when the code continues running.>
functions.builtin5:
Last but not least we will take a look at the scrollTo() built-in function:
The scrollTo() function is a JavaScript method that allows you to scroll the web page to a specific position. Here is an example of how you can use this function:
functions.builtin6: In this example, the scrollTo() function is called twice. The first time it is called, it scrolls the page to the top of the document by setting the x and y coordinates to 0. The second time it is called, it scrolls the page to the bottom of the document by setting the x coordinate to 0 and the y coordinate to the total height of the document (which can be accessed using the document.body.scrollHeight property).
functions.builtin7: You can also specify a specific position to scroll to by providing the x and y coordinates of the position you want to scroll to 👇
functions.builtin8: In this case, the page will be scrolled so that the position at (100, 200) is at the top-left corner of the visible part of the page.
functions.builtin9: The scrollTo() function is useful for creating web pages that have interactive elements, such as buttons or links that allow the user to quickly and easily navigate to specific parts of the page. It can also be used to create animation effects, such as smoothly scrolling the page to a new position when a button is clicked.
functions.custom1:
A custom JavaScript function is a function that you define yourself, rather than one that is built-in to the JavaScript language. You can create custom functions to perform any tasks that you need to in your code, such as calculating the area of a rectangle or formatting a date.
To create a custom function in JavaScript, you first need to use the function keyword, followed by the name of your function 👇
functions.custom2: In this example, the function is named calculateArea and it takes two arguments, width and height, which represent the dimensions of a rectangle. Inside the function, you can write any code that you want to be executed when the function is called.
functions.custom3: The buzzwords here are you either create, define or call a function down the road. There are different ways to define a function, and we'll look into the first one: In the above example we just defined our first function called calculateArea. So the synstax in the first method that you type in the function keyword and the right after that the function name, followed by () and then by a {} in which you declare a function block. Everything inside the function block is called the function body.
functions.custom4: Once we have defined our custom function you are also able to call the function just like you would start any other JavaScript function:
functions.custom5: In this case, the calculateArea function will be called and it will be passed the arguments 10 and 20. The code inside the function will be executed, and the result of the calculation will be returned. You can also use the return statement to specify the value that the function should return. For example:
functions.custom6: In this case, the calculateArea function calculates the area of a rectangle by multiplying the width and height arguments, and then it returns the result of the calculation using the return statement. Custom functions can make your code more organized and reusable, and they can help you avoid repeating the same code in multiple places in your program. You can use custom functions to break down complex tasks into smaller, more manageable pieces of code, which can make your code easier to read and understand.
functions.custom7: In JavaScript, you can store the value that is returned from a function in a variable. To do this, you simply need to assign the value that is returned from the function to a variable using the assignment operator (=). Here is an example of how you can store the value returned from a function in a variable:
functions.custom8: In this example, the getRandomNumber function returns a random number using the Math.random() function. The value that is returned is then stored in the randomNumber variable. Once the value has been stored in a variable, you can use it just like any other variable in your code.
functions.custom9: Storing the value returned from a function in a variable is a common technique in JavaScript, and it can be useful for many different purposes. For example, you might use it to save the result of a calculation, to store user input, or to temporarily hold a value that you will use later in your code.
functions.parametersArguments1:
In JavaScript, function parameters are the names listed in the function definition, while arguments are the actual values passed to the function when it is called.
Here is an example of a function with two parameters, a and b:
functions.parametersArguments2:
In this code, the add() function is called with the arguments 1 and 2, which are assigned to the a and b parameters, respectively. The function then returns the sum of a and b, which is 3.
Function parameters are essentially placeholders for the values that are passed to the function when it is called. They allow us to write functions that can be used with different sets of arguments without having to change the function definition.
functions.parametersArguments3:
Arguments, on the other hand, are the actual values that are passed to the function when it is called. In the example above, the arguments 1 and 2 are passed to the add() function and are used to calculate the result.
In JavaScript, it is possible to define default values for function parameters. This means that if an argument is not passed to the function, the default value will be used instead. For example, consider the following function that takes two arguments and returns their product, with a default value of 1 for the second argument 👇
functions.parametersArguments4: In this code, the multiply() function is called with only one argument, 5. Since the b parameter has a default value of 1, it is assigned the value 1 and the function returns the product of a and b, which is 5.
functions.parametersArguments5: Overall, parameters and arguments are important concepts in JavaScript that allow us to write functions that can be used with different sets of arguments. Parameters are the placeholders for the arguments that are passed to the function, while arguments are the actual values that are passed to the function when it is called.
functions.differentWays1: One common thing you will read is that Functions are first-class citizens. In JavaScript, a first-class citizen (also known as 'first-class objects') is a term used to refer to functions that are treated as first-class members of the language. This means that they can be assigned to variables, passed as arguments to functions, and returned as values from functions, just like any other type of data (strings, numbers, etc.). This is an important feature of the language that allows for the creation of flexible and modular code.
functions.differentWays2: In JavaScript, an anonymous function is a function that has no name. It is defined and called inline, without any reference to a named function. Anonymous functions are often used as arguments to other functions, or as a way to define a function on the fly without the need to give it a name. Here is an example of an anonymous function being passed as an argument to the 'setTimeout' function, which will call the function after a specified number of milliseconds 👇
functions.differentWays3: In this example, the anonymous function is passed as an argument to setTimeout, and will be called after a one-second delay. The function simply logs a message to the console, but it could be used to perform any arbitrary action.
functions.differentWays4: A function expression is a way of defining a function using a variable assignment. This means that you can assign a function to a variable, and then use that variable as a function. Function expressions are often used to define anonymous functions, which are functions that are not bound to a named identifier. Here is an example of a function expression that defines an anonymous function and assigns it to the variable sayHello:
functions.differentWays5: In this example, the anonymous function is defined using the function keyword, and is assigned to the variable sayHello. The function can then be called by referencing the sayHello variable. Function expressions are a powerful and flexible feature of JavaScript that allow for the creation of modular and reusable code.
functions.differentWays6: The main difference between a function declaration and a function expression is in how they are defined and used in JavaScript code. Function declarations are defined using the function keyword, followed by the function name and the function body.
functions.differentWays7: Function declarations are hoisted, which means that they are available to be called throughout the entire script, even before they are defined. This means that you can call a function declaration before it appears in the code. More on that later but here is a quick example:
functions.differentWays8: In contrast, function expressions are defined using a variable assignment, with the function keyword being used to define an anonymous function. Function expressions are not hoisted, which means that they are not available to be called until after they are defined in the code. This means that you cannot call a function expression before it appears in the code 👇
functions.differentWays9: Another key difference between function declarations and function expressions is that function declarations must have a name, whereas function expressions can be anonymous. This means that you cannot define a named function using a function expression, like this:
functions.differentWays10:
In summary, the main differences between function declarations and function expressions are:


  • 👉 Function declarations are defined using the function keyword, followed by the function name and function body. Function expressions are defined using a variable assignment, with the function keyword defining an anonymous function
  • 👉 Function declarations are hoisted, which means that they are available to be called throughout the entire script. Function expressions are not hoisted, which means that they are not available to be called until after they are defined in the code
  • 👉 Function declarations must have a name, whereas function expressions can be anonymous
functions.arrowFunctions1: In JavaScript, an arrow function is a more concise way to write a function expression. It uses the fat arrow syntax (=>) to create a function without the function keyword. Arrow functions are often used in conjunction with array methods like map() and filter() to transform and filter data. For example, here is a simple function that takes two numbers and returns their sum and we could rewrite it like this:
functions.arrowFunctions2: If the function only has one argument, you can omit the parentheses around the argument list. If the function only has one statement, you can also omit the curly braces and the return keyword:
functions.arrowFunctions3:
In this case, the square function takes one argument x and returns the square of x.
One of the main advantages of using arrow functions is that they have a shorter syntax than regular function expressions. This can make your code more readable and easier to write. Additionally, arrow functions do NOT have their own this value, which means they can be used to write more concise code that works with this in the containing scope.
functions.callbackFunctions1: In JavaScript, a callback function is a function that is passed as an argument to another function and is executed after some kind of event or operation has occurred. Callback functions are a common pattern in JavaScript and are used to handle asynchronous operations such as user input, network requests, and timers. An example of a callback function handling a button click event 👇
functions.callbackFunctions2: In this code, the anonymous arrow function () => { console.log('The button was clicked!') } is passed as an argument to the addEventListener() method, which is called when the user clicks the button.
functions.callbackFunctions3: Another common use of callback functions is to handle asynchronous operations. For example, the setTimeout() function takes a callback function that is executed after a specified amount of time has passed:
functions.callbackFunctions4:
In this code, the anonymous arrow function () => { console.log('Hello, world!') } is passed as an argument to the setTimeout() function and is executed after one second has passed.
In general, callback functions are a powerful tool for handling asynchronous operations in JavaScript, and are commonly used in a wide range of applications.
